mappings.rationaleAI: Directly addresses cell theory from Hooke's 1665 observations onward, the diversity of cell types (neurons, muscle fibers, immune cells) in the human body, cell organelle structure, and cell division processes including mitosis and meiosis, spanning virtually the entire section outline.

Proteins, nucleic acids (DNA), polysaccharides, and lipids are the macromolecular and small-molecule constituents of cells, directly mapping to the cell as a molecular system including both macromolecular and small-molecule components.

Mitochondria are membrane-bound organelles central to cell energetics with their own DNA, directly relevant as morphological elements of the cell; the endosymbiotic theory of their origin from engulfed bacteria illustrates the interplay between nucleus and cytoplasm in eukaryotic cell evolution.

Enzymes control the speed of all chemical reactions within cells, regulate cell growth and programmed cell death, and enable intercellular communication, making them fundamental to the functional aspects of cells and the cell growth phase of the cell cycle.

The discovery of archaea as a separate domain distinct from bacteria, sharing cellular features with eukaryotes, and the theory that bacterial-archaeal fusion produced eukaryotic cells are directly relevant to the classification and comparison of prokaryotic and eukaryotic cell types.

Free radicals as byproducts of cellular respiration that cause cumulative intracellular damage relate to the cell's internal environment, while their positive roles in cell signalling and driving normal cell division connect to the cell growth and division cycle.

Slime moulds exhibit amoeboid cell movement and collective problem-solving through cellular aggregation, directly illustrating both cell motility and how individual cells combine, communicate, and form cohesive multicellular structures without nervous systems.

Franklin's X-ray crystallography of DNA, including Photograph 51, was pivotal to determining the double-helix structure of nucleic acids, which are among the principal macromolecules constituting the cell's molecular system.
